_what_this_is: Content request: suggested workout programs behind Train's program chips (PPL, Upper/Lower, Bro, Full Body). Each program offers one or more variants by training days per week; selecting a program shows its weekly schedule and lets the user adopt it as their plan.
_sourcing_notes: Exercise selection and set/rep/rest prescriptions follow widely-used strength & hypertrophy programming conventions: compound lifts are placed first in each session while recovery is highest, typically in the 6-10 rep range at 90-180s rest for strength-hypertrophy blend; isolation/accessory work follows at 10-15 reps with 45-75s rest, standard for hypertrophy-focused accessory training.

Several main lifts (the first exercise of most days) use repsPerSet as an ascending-load pyramid (e.g. [10,8,6,6]) rather than a flat rep target, reflecting how these lifts are commonly programmed in real training (moderate warm-up-adjacent sets leading into a heavier top set), and to demonstrate the repsPerSet field working end-to-end.
All exerciseId values were validated against content/muscle-involvement_filled.json (the 189-exercise catalog) — every id below exists in that file.
PPL 5-day variant intentionally drops one leg day (keeping Legs A only) rather than trying to force two full PPL cycles into 5 days, since that is how this variant is commonly run in practice.

Full Body 2-day variant is framed as a maintenance/time-constrained option rather than a primary muscle-building layout, since 2 full-body sessions a week gives each pattern lower total weekly frequency than the 3-day version.

SCHEMA V2 + THREE NEW PROGRAMS (authored in-repo): reps may now be a number, a repsPerSet pyramid, or the string 'failure' (AMRAP); timed work uses durationSeconds (the old Plank 'reps: 45' was seconds in disguise and is fixed). Every program carries a level. Body Part Split (4-day) follows classic aesthetic-split convention — machine/cable-forward isolation after one barbell anchor per day, each muscle pair owning a full session; it differs from the Bro Split by pairing chest+triceps / back+biceps (one less day, shoulders still get their own). Fat Loss Focus alternates full-body/upper/lower lifting with two circuit conditioning days built from the catalog's functional entries (swings, sled, rope, rower, carries) per concurrent-training practice: resistance work preserves lean mass in a deficit while intervals drive energy expenditure. Arnold Split reflects the documented Chest&Back / Shoulders&Arms / Legs&LowerBack double-run week at 3-4 sets of 'up to 10' — mapped naturally onto targets-plus-failure; abs at 5x25 as he ran them.
_how_to_fill: For each program, provide at least one variant. A variant = a specific days-per-week layout (e.g. PPL as a 6-day and a 5-day variant).
recommendedDaysPerWeek: which weekly frequencies this program suits — used to recommend a program from the user's onboarding answer (e.g. Upper/Lower suits 4, PPL suits 5-6).
Each day: a name and an ordered exercise list. exerciseId MUST be an id from content/muscle-involvement_filled.json (the 189-exercise catalog).

sets: number of sets. reps: target per set (number), repsPerSet: pyramid array, reps: "failure" for AMRAP sets, or durationSeconds for timed holds/carries/intervals. Every rep number is a TARGET, never a cap — training past it to failure is always valid.
Optional per day: focus (one line shown under the day name, e.g. 'Chest, shoulders, triceps').
Optional per variant: notes (one or two lines of coaching guidance shown with the schedule).
Fill the four presets. The 'Coach' program is generated in-app and needs no content.

programs.name: Push Pull Legs
programs.description: A high-frequency split that trains each movement pattern twice per week, built for lifters who want to prioritize hypertrophy and can commit to 5-6 sessions weekly.

variants.notes: Each muscle group is trained twice per week. Run Heavy Push / Heavy Pull / Power Legs one cycle, then Shoulder Push / Width Pull / Hip Power the next, back to back with one rest day per week wherever it fits your schedule.

A single weekly leg session with two push and two pull sessions. Good for lifters who want most of the PPL benefit without training 6 days a week — rotate which leg day (Power Legs or Hip Power) you use week to week.

programs.name: Upper / Lower
programs.description: A balanced 4-day split alternating upper and lower body sessions — enough frequency to drive progress without the daily commitment of PPL or a bro split.

variants.notes: Run Upper Strength, Lower Strength, Upper Volume, Lower Volume across the week with rest days spaced between, e.g. Mon/Tue/Thu/Fri. Strength days lean heavier and lower-rep; Volume days add sets at moderate reps.

programs.name: Bro Split
programs.description: One muscle group per day for maximum focused volume and pump — a classic bodybuilding-style split for lifters training 5 days a week who enjoy specializing session to session.

variants.notes: Run Chest, Back, Shoulders, Arms, Legs in order across the week, e.g. Mon-Fri with the weekend off. Each muscle group is trained once weekly, so push volume and intensity hard within each session.

programs.name: Full Body
programs.description: Every major movement pattern trained each session — ideal for beginners building a base, or anyone who can only commit to 2-3 sessions a week and wants full-body coverage every time.

variants.notes: Run Squat & Press, Hinge & Pull, Press & Row in order with a rest day between each session, e.g. Mon/Wed/Fri. Each day covers a squat or hinge pattern, a push, a pull, and core work, so no single day is a 'leg day' or 'chest day' — the whole body is hit every time.

days.name: Press & Row
days.focus: Leg press pattern, incline push, row

variants.notes: For a 2-day-per-week schedule, alternate Squat & Press and Hinge & Pull week to week so each session still covers a full-body pattern. Best suited to maintenance or a very time-constrained schedule rather than active muscle building.

programs.name: Body Part Split
programs.description: Four focused days — each muscle pair gets a full session of volume with machine and cable work for clean isolation. The 4-day cousin of the Bro Split: one less gym day, shoulders get their own session, and legs are never squeezed out.

variants.notes: Spread the four days across the week with rest wherever life needs it — e.g. Mon/Tue/Thu/Sat. Rep numbers are targets, not caps: taking the last set of any isolation past the target to failure is exactly how this split is meant to be run.

days.name: Chest & Triceps
days.focus: Pressing volume, then triceps to finish

days.name: Shoulders
days.focus: All three delt heads, traps, core to close

programs.name: Fat Loss Focus
programs.description: Strength and conditioning woven together: lifting preserves muscle while the engine days drive the calorie burn. Built for weight loss and general fitness — high energy, never boring.

variants.notes: Alternate lifting and conditioning: strength Mon, engine Tue, upper Wed, engine Thu, lower Fri. Conditioning days run circuit-style — the listed rest is between exercises; keep moving inside each round. Pair with a modest calorie deficit; the training protects the muscle.

days.name: Full Body Strength
days.focus: Big patterns, moderate loads — muscle is what keeps the burn

days.name: Circuit Burner
days.focus: Circuit style — move briskly, rest fully only between rounds

days.name: Interval Engine
days.focus: Intervals — hard minute, easy recovery, repeat

days.name: Lower Body
days.focus: Legs and glutes with short rests

variants.notes: The time-constrained version: one conditioning day instead of two. Keep walks or easy cardio on off days if fat loss is the priority.

days.name: Full Body Strength
days.focus: Big patterns, moderate loads — muscle is what keeps the burn

days.name: Circuit Burner
days.focus: Circuit style — move briskly, rest fully only between rounds

days.name: Lower Body
days.focus: Legs and glutes with short rests

programs.name: Arnold Split
programs.description: The six-day split from Arnold's competition years: chest with back, shoulders with arms, legs with lower back — each pairing hit twice a week at heroic volume. For experienced lifters with recovery to spare.

variants.notes: Every rep count is an 'up to' target in Arnold's own style — the last set of anything can go to failure. The split is famously flexible: swap movements for your equipment and joints (chin-ups → lat pulldown, barbell press → dumbbell press) as long as the pattern stays. Sunday off.

days.name: Chest & Back
days.focus: Antagonist pairing — press and pull in one session

days.name: Legs & Lower Back
days.focus: Squat, hinge, and the posterior chain

days.name: Chest & Back
days.focus: Antagonist pairing — press and pull in one session

days.name: Legs & Lower Back
days.focus: Squat, hinge, and the posterior chain

_substitutions: Arnold: Barbell Clean & Press -> ex-db-clean-and-press (no barbell clean-and-press in catalog; ex-clean-and-jerk is the Olympic lift, too technical for this slot).
Arnold: Seated Dumbbell Curl -> ex-db-curl (standing; closest catalog curl — ex-incline-db-curl also acceptable).
Arnold: Standing Barbell Triceps Extension -> ex-overhead-triceps-ext (dumbbell overhead extension, same overhead-triceps pattern).
Arnold: Reverse Wrist Curl -> ex-reverse-curl (reverse-grip curl loads the same wrist extensors; a dedicated ex-reverse-wrist-curl would be the ideal future catalog addition).
Fat Loss: 'burpee' has no catalog entry — conditioning circuits use mountain climbers / jump rope / thrusters instead; ex-burpee is a suggested future catalog addition.
